skill.name: network-tox-docking-research-planner
skill.description: Generates complete network toxicology and molecular docking research designs from a user-provided toxicant and disease combination. Outputs four workload configurations (Lite, Standard, Advanced, Publication+) with step-by-step workflows, figure plans, validation strategies, and publication upgrade paths.
skill.summary: Designs structured network toxicology and molecular docking research plans for studying environmental toxicant-disease mechanisms.
skill.author: AIPOCH

security_audit.summary: All 53 static findings are FALSE POSITIVES after AI evaluation. The skill is a text-only research design planner with no executable code, network calls, or credential access. Weak cryptographic algorithm findings refer to statistical significance thresholds (p.adjust, q.value) and database score cutoffs in bioinformatics contexts, not cryptographic functions. Shell backtick execution findings are markdown code formatting for file paths and tool names. The skill has strong scientific integrity guardrails including Hard Rules 4-6 preventing causal overclaiming and passed 34/34 dynamic assertions rated Production Ready (96.8/100).

high_findings.title: Weak Cryptographic Algorithm References
high_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE: 30+ static findings flagged references to statistical significance thresholds, database score cutoffs, and confidence parameters as weak cryptographic algorithms. All instances refer to bioinformatics parameters such as p.adjust < 0.05 (statistical significance), q < 0.05 (FDR correction), DisGeNET GDA score >= 0.1 (gene-disease association cutoff), GeneCards relevance score >= 1 (gene ranking), and STRING confidence >= 0.7 (protein interaction confidence). These are standard bioinformatics analytical thresholds, not cryptographic algorithm usage. No actual cryptographic operations are performed by this skill.

high_findings.title: Ruby or Shell Backtick Execution
high_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE: 14 static findings flagged backtick-enclosed text as Ruby or shell command execution. All instances are markdown code formatting syntax used to denote file paths (e.g., 'references/decision-logic.md'), module names, tool names, and R package identifiers. This is standard markdown documentation practice for inline code references. No actual shell command execution exists anywhere in this skill.

high_findings.title: Critical Heuristic: Code Execution Plus Network Plus Credential Access
high_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE: The static analyzer flagged a heuristic combination of code execution, network access, and credential access patterns. This aggregate finding is based entirely on individual false positives listed above (markdown backticks misidentified as shell execution, bioinformatics parameter references misidentified as crypto, and a benign GitHub URL citation). There is no actual code execution, no network connectivity, and no credential access in this skill. The skill generates text-only research design plans.

medium_findings.title: SQLite Database File Reference
medium_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE: Static finding at references/modules.md:29 flagged 'SQLite database file.' The file lists R/Bioconductor packages used for enrichment analysis including org.Hs.eg.db, which is a Bioconductor annotation database package name (human genome orgDb), not an SQLite database file. No actual SQLite database is present, opened, or accessed by this skill.

low_findings.title: Python HTTP Libraries Reference
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE: Static finding at eval_report.json:69 flagged 'Python HTTP libraries.' The eval report discusses bioinformatics web resources (CTD, SwissTargetPrediction, GeneCards, STRING, CB-Dock2) in the context of research design recommendations. These are mentioned as target data sources for a study, not as actual HTTP calls made by the skill. The skill generates text plans only and does not make any network requests.

low_findings.title: System Reconnaissance Reference
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE: Two static findings flagged 'system reconnaissance' at references/configurations.md:46 and references/study-patterns.md:10. The configurations.md reference is in a reviewer risk register about 'Weak transcriptomic validation (small GEO cohort).' The study-patterns.md reference is a study design pattern description. Neither relates to system reconnaissance.

low_findings.title: Network Reconnaissance Reference
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE: Static finding at references/decision-logic.md:12 flagged 'network reconnaissance.' The file section lists toxicant classes (EDC, heavy metal, food contaminant, pharmaceutical residue) for study pattern selection. This is about toxicant classification, not network scanning or reconnaissance.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URL in SKILL.md
low_findings.description: TRUE POSITIVE but benign: SKILL.md:7 contains a GitHub repository URL as a source citation for the skill. This is a legitimate reference link to the author's medical-research-skills repository. No obfuscation or exfiltration pattern present.

content.user_title: Design Network Toxicology and Molecular Docking Studies
content.value_statement: Researchers need structured protocols for studying how environmental toxicants contribute to disease mechanisms. This skill produces complete, tiered research designs with target prediction, network analysis, and docking workflows ready for execution.
actual_capabilities: Generates four tiered research designs (Lite, Standard, Advanced, Publication+) with workload estimates
Produces toxicant target prediction and disease target retrieval workflows with database source recommendations
Designs PPI network construction and hub gene identification protocols with algorithm selection
Creates molecular docking study plans with target selection logic and structure requirements
Includes validation strategies with five evidence layers and explicit proves-versus-does-not-prove statements
Provides publication upgrade paths, reviewer risk mitigation, and figure deliverable lists
limitations: Does not execute any analysis or run any bioinformatics software tools
Cannot access external databases (CTD, GeneCards, STRING) or retrieve real data
Cannot perform docking simulations or generate binding energy values
Requires a specific toxicant and disease or phenotype as input

use_cases.title: Environmental Health Researcher
use_cases.description: Design a study on how a food contaminant affects organ toxicity using network toxicology and molecular docking methods.
use_cases.target_user: Academic researcher in toxicology or environmental health

use_cases.title: Graduate Student Planning Thesis Work
use_cases.description: Create a structured research plan for a BPA and endometriosis mechanism study with clear milestones and publication targets.
use_cases.target_user: PhD or MSc student in pharmacology or toxicology

use_cases.title: Manuscript Reviewer
use_cases.description: Evaluate whether a submitted toxicology study design follows methodological best practices and proper evidence standards.
use_cases.target_user: Journal peer reviewer or editorial board member

prompt_templates.title: Basic Toxicology Study Design
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a network toxicology and molecular docking study for [toxicant] and [disease].
prompt_templates.scenario: Starting a new research project with a known toxicant-disease pair.

prompt_templates.title: Comparative Multi-Toxicant Analysis
prompt_templates.prompt: Compare how [toxicant1], [toxicant2], and [toxicant3] may contribute to [disease] through shared and unique molecular targets.
prompt_templates.scenario: Investigating multiple pollutants linked to the same disease outcome.

prompt_templates.title: Publication-Oriented Full Pipeline
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a Publication+ tier research design for [toxicant] and [disease] targeting a high-impact journal. Include multi-source target harmonization, dual validation datasets, and wet-lab follow-up suggestions.
prompt_templates.scenario: Preparing a comprehensive manuscript for competitive journal submission.

prompt_templates.title: Time-Constrained Minimal Study
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a Lite tier network toxicology study for [toxicant] and [disease] with a 2-week deadline and public data only.
prompt_templates.scenario: Rapid preliminary analysis for grant proposals or conference abstracts.

output_examples.input: Design a network toxicology and molecular docking study for triclosan and osteoporosis.
output_examples.output: A structured research plan with four workload tiers, recommended Standard plan, step-by-step workflow for each phase, target prediction sources including CTD and SwissTargetPrediction, PPI construction parameters, hub gene identification via CytoHubba MCC algorithm, GO and KEGG enrichment, docking to top hub targets, figure deliverable list, and validation strategy with explicit proves and does not prove statements for each evidence layer.

output_examples.input: Compare chlorpyrifos, atrazine, and glyphosate for their roles in cardiovascular disease.
output_examples.output: A comparative multi-toxicant study design with independent per-toxicant target retrieval, shared and unique target identification via comparative Venn diagram, per-toxicant hub gene table, comparative docking score table, explicit constraint against synergy claims, and pattern-specific deliverables including three-set overlap analysis.

best_practices: Specify both a toxicant and a disease or phenotype to generate a complete research design with all required sections.
Use the recommended tier as your starting point and review the upgrade path if targeting a higher-impact journal.
Always use two or more target prediction sources to reduce database-specific noise in toxicant target identification.
anti_patterns: Requesting the skill to execute analysis steps or run bioinformatics tools such as STRING or CB-Dock2 directly.
Treating hub genes or docking results as proven causal drivers without acknowledging the need for experimental validation.
Asking a single-toxicant question when the goal is multi-toxicant comparison without specifying all toxicants upfront.

faq.question: Does this skill run any bioinformatics analysis for me?
faq.answer: No. It generates a research design plan only. You must execute the analysis separately using tools such as STRING, Cytoscape, and CB-Dock2.

faq.question: What input do I need to provide?
faq.answer: You need at least one toxicant name and one disease or phenotype name. Optionally specify a workload tier (Lite, Standard, Advanced, Publication+).

faq.question: What is the difference between the four workload tiers?
faq.answer: Lite is a 2 to 4 week skeleton plan. Standard is a 4 to 6 week publication-ready design. Advanced adds multiple databases and sensitivity analysis over 6 to 10 weeks. Publication+ includes multi-layer validation and wet-lab suggestions over 10 to 16 weeks.

faq.question: Can I compare multiple toxicants in one study?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill supports multi-toxicant comparative analysis for up to three toxicants. It runs independent target retrieval for each toxicant before comparison.

faq.question: Does docking analysis prove that a chemical causes disease?
faq.answer: No. Docking shows computational binding feasibility only. It does not prove in vivo binding or biological activity. The skill explicitly separates computational evidence from experimental proof.

faq.question: What happens if there is no overlap between toxicant and disease targets?
faq.answer: The skill activates a five-step recovery sequence: loosening database filters, adding prediction sources, manual literature curation, pathway-level analysis, and infeasibility declaration with alternative recommendations.
